title: Synthetic Data Generation — Training, Evaluation & Alignment at Scale
description: Synthetic data is AI-generated training data used to augment, replace, or bootstrap real datasets. At FAANG Staff+ level, synthetic data is the backbone of post-training (RLHF, DPO), evaluation (benchmark generation), and data augmentation (low-resource domains). You must design generation pipelines that produce high-quality, diverse, and minimally biased synthetic data at scale — while avoiding model collapse, hallucination contamination, and distribution shift.

sections.title: The Role of Synthetic Data in LLM Development
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Synthetic data is not a replacement for real data — it's a force multiplier. The three critical use cases are: Post-training alignment (RLHF/DPO): you need preference pairs (chosen vs rejected responses) for thousands of prompts. Human annotators are slow and expensive. Synthetic preference data from a strong model (GPT-4, Claude) matches or exceeds human-written preferences at 10x lower cost. Evaluation benchmarks: you need eval sets with known ground truth. Synthetically generated Q&A pairs with controlled difficulty levels give you calibrated evaluations. Data augmentation: you have 10K real examples but need 100K for fine-tuning. Synthetic examples generated by paraphrasing, back-translation, or style transfer fill the gap. The Staff insight: synthetic data quality > quantity. 10K high-quality synthetic examples beat 100K noisy ones. The generation pipeline is the product — invest in prompt design, filtering, and diversity sampling.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Synthetic preference data: generating 100K (prompt, chosen, rejected) triples with GPT-4 costs ~$5K (at $0.05 per triple). Human annotation of the same: $50K-100K. Quality: synthetic preferences achieve 85-90% agreement with human preferences (Anthropic's Constitutional AI). Evaluation benchmarks: generating 10K Q&A pairs with controlled difficulty costs ~$2K. Model collapse (Shumailov et al., 2023): training exclusively on synthetic data causes quality degradation after 5-10 generations (models learn to mimic the synthetic distribution, losing tail diversity). Mixing 10-30% real data prevents collapse. Self-play (Go, chess): AlphaGo Zero trained entirely on self-play data achieved superhuman performance — but self-play works best in closed, verifiable domains.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Define the data requirement: task, format, diversity requirements, validation criteria. 2) Design a generation prompt template with: role, task description, output format, quality criteria, and diversity constraints. 3) Generate candidate examples with a strong teacher model (GPT-4o, Claude 3.5). 4) Filter and validate: remove low-quality examples using a judge model, check for hallucinations, check diversity (embedding similarity). 5) Mix with real data: 70-90% synthetic + 10-30% real. 6) Iterate: train a small model on the synthetic data, evaluate on a held-out real test set, improve the generation pipeline. 7) Monitor for distribution shift: compare synthetic data distribution to real data distribution (MMD, KL divergence).

search Interview Angle: "You need to fine-tune a model for a medical Q&A task but only have 500 real doctor-patient Q&A pairs. How do you generate synthetic data to scale to 50K pairs?" Key: use GPT-4 to generate diverse medical questions (taxonomy-driven: disease categories, symptom types, treatment options), generate answers with citations from medical textbooks, validate with a medical NLI model, mix with real data.
sections.codeBlock:
# Synthetic data generation pipeline with quality filtering
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional
import hashlib, json, random

@dataclass
class SyntheticExample:
    prompt: str
    chosen: str  # preferred response
    rejected: str  # dispreferred response
    source_model: str
    quality_score: float  # 0-1, from judge model
    diversity_cluster: int  # cluster ID for diversity sampling
    metadata: dict = field(default_factory=dict)


class SyntheticDataPipeline:
    """
    End-to-end synthetic data generation pipeline.
    Generates, filters, and samples diverse training data.
    """
    def __init__(self, teacher_model="gpt-4o",
                 judge_model="gpt-4o-mini"):
        self.teacher_model = teacher_model
        self.judge_model = judge_model
        self.examples: list[SyntheticExample] = []

    def generate_prompts(self, seed_topics: list[str],
                         count: int = 1000) -> list[str]:
        """Generate diverse prompts from seed topics."""
        prompt_template = """Generate {count} diverse prompts for an AI assistant.
        The prompts should cover these topics: {topics}
        
        Requirements:
        - Each prompt should be a realistic user request
        - Cover easy, medium, and hard difficulty levels
        - Include open-ended, factual, and creative prompts
        - Avoid repetition: vary sentence structure and vocabulary
        - Length: 10-100 words per prompt
        
        Return a JSON array of strings."""
        
        response = self._call_teacher(
            prompt_template.format(count=count, topics=", ".join(seed_topics))
        )
        try:
            return json.loads(response)
        except json.JSONDecodeError:
            return []

    def generate_preference_pair(self, prompt: str) -> SyntheticExample:
        """Generate a (chosen, rejected) preference pair."""
        # Generate two responses
        chosen = self._call_teacher(
            prompt, system="You are a helpful, accurate assistant."
        )
        rejected = self._call_teacher(
            prompt, system="You are a careless assistant. "
                           "Make up facts, be verbose but unhelpful."
        )
        
        # Quality score from judge
        quality = self._judge_quality(prompt, chosen, rejected)
        
        return SyntheticExample(
            prompt=prompt,
            chosen=chosen,
            rejected=rejected,
            source_model=self.teacher_model,
            quality_score=quality,
            diversity_cluster=0
        )

    def filter_by_quality(self, threshold: float = 0.7):
        """Remove low-quality examples."""
        before = len(self.examples)
        self.examples = [e for e in self.examples
                         if e.quality_score >= threshold]
        return {"before": before, "after": len(self.examples),
                "removed": before - len(self.examples)}

    def sample_diverse(self, max_count: int, similarity_threshold: float = 0.85):
        """Diversity sampling: pick examples from different semantic clusters."""
        if len(self.examples) <= max_count:
            return self.examples
        
        # Compute embeddings and cluster
        # In production: use sentence-transformers or text-embedding-3
        selected = []
        seen_hashes = set()
        
        for example in self.examples:
            # Simple hash-based dedup for demonstration
            content_hash = hashlib.md5(
                example.prompt.encode()
            ).hexdigest()[:16]
            if content_hash not in seen_hashes:
                selected.append(example)
                seen_hashes.add(content_hash)
            if len(selected) >= max_count:
                break
        
        return selected

    def mix_with_real(self, synthetic: list, real: list,
                      synthetic_ratio: float = 0.8) -> list:
        """Mix synthetic and real data."""
        real_count = int(len(synthetic) * (1 - synthetic_ratio) / synthetic_ratio)
        real_sample = random.sample(real, min(len(real), real_count))
        combined = synthetic + real_sample
        random.shuffle(combined)
        return combined

    def _call_teacher(self, prompt: str, system: str = ""
                      ) -> str:
        """Call the teacher LLM."""
        # In production: actual API call
        return f"Response to: {prompt[:50]}..."

    def _judge_quality(self, prompt: str, chosen: str,
                       rejected: str) -> float:
        """Judge the quality of a preference pair."""
        return random.uniform(0.5, 1.0)  # Placeholder

sections.title: Preference Data: RLHF & DPO at Scale
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Post-training alignment (RLHF, DPO, Constitutional AI) requires large datasets of preference pairs: given a prompt, which of two responses is better? Human annotation is expensive, slow, and inconsistent across annotators. Synthetic preference data generation solves this by using a strong, aligned model to generate both the chosen and rejected responses. The key techniques: Constitutional AI (Anthropic) generates preference pairs by having the model critique and revise its own outputs based on a constitution (a set of principles). Self-play has the model generate multiple responses and pick the best via self-evaluation. Adversarial generation uses a separate model that tries to generate rejected responses by violating the desired criteria. The Staff insight: the choice of rejection sampling strategy is more important than the number of examples. Bias in the rejection strategy creates bias in the final model.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Anthropic's Constitutional AI: 150K synthetic preference pairs via self-critique. Llama 2: 1M human-preference annotations from Meta contractors ($2M budget). Direct Preference Optimisation (DPO): needs ~10K-50K preference pairs for effective alignment (vs 100K+ for RLHF). Quality floor: preference pairs with <70% agreement with human judges harm model performance. Generation speed: GPT-4 generates ~2 preference pairs/second, 500K pairs = ~70 hours, cost ~$25K. Diversity: synthetic preference data from a single model tends to over-represent the model's own style — mixing responses from different models (GPT-4, Claude, Gemini) improves diversity by 40%.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Define the desired behaviour in a constitution or rubric (5-10 principles). 2) Generate a diverse set of prompts covering the target use cases. 3) For each prompt, use the teacher model to generate two responses (chosen = follows constitution, rejected = violates constitution). 4) Use the teacher model to self-critique and improve both responses. 5) Validate: sample 1% of pairs for human review, measure agreement rate. 6) Train a reward model on the synthetic pairs (if using RLHF) or directly apply DPO. 7) Evaluate: compare model behaviour on a held-out set of red-teaming prompts before and after alignment. 8) Iterate: if the model exhibits unwanted behaviours, add those as new rejection generation strategies.

sections.title: Synthetic Evaluation Benchmarks: Calibrated Testing at Scale
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Evaluation benchmarks are the lens through which you measure model quality. Real benchmarks (MMLU, HumanEval, GSM8K) are static — they leak into training data over time. Synthetic benchmarks solve this by generating fresh, controlled test sets on demand. You can control difficulty (easy/medium/hard), domain (medicine, law, coding), and format (MCQ, free-form, code). The killer feature: synthetic benchmarks with verifiable answers. For factual questions, you can extract the answer from a trusted source and verify the model's response programmatically. The Staff insight: a synthetic benchmark is only as good as its generation pipeline. The same failures that affect models affect synthetic data generation — hallucinations in the generator propagate to the benchmark, making the model look wrong when it's actually right.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Synthetic benchmark contamination: <5% overlap with real training data (vs 30-50% for MMLU). Difficulty calibration: GPT-4 achieves 90% on easy synthetic questions, 65% on medium, 35% on hard — useful for regression testing. Generation cost: 10K synthetic Q&A pairs with verifiable answers = ~$500 (GPT-4o). Human-written equivalent: $5K-10K. Benchmark stability: synthetic benchmarks show 2-3% variance across 5 different generation runs (use multiple seeds and average). Agreement with human-written benchmarks: 0.85-0.95 correlation (Spearman) for model rankings. Synthetic code benchmarks (HumanEval-style): pass@1 rates correlate at r=0.92 with real code benchmarks.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Define the capability you want to measure: factual knowledge, reasoning, code generation, safety. 2) Create a taxonomy of sub-skills with difficulty levels. 3) For each (sub-skill, difficulty), write generation templates. 4) Generate questions with a strong model. 5) For factual questions, verify answers against a trusted knowledge base (Wikipedia, textbooks). 6) For reasoning questions, verify step-by-step logic or use a verifier model. 7) Store the benchmark with versioned metadata (generation date, model, prompt template). 8) Run the benchmark weekly, track regression. 9) If a model regresses, generate additional examples targeting the regression area for fine-tuning.
sections.codeBlock:
# Synthetic benchmark generation with verifiable answers
from typing import Optional
import json, random, hashlib

class SyntheticBenchmark:
    """
    Generates synthetic evaluation benchmarks with verifiable answers.
    Supports multiple domains, difficulty levels, and answer formats.
    """
    def __init__(self, teacher_model="gpt-4o",
                 knowledge_base: Optional[dict] = None):
        self.teacher_model = teacher_model
        self.knowledge_base = knowledge_base or {}
        self.questions: list[dict] = []

    def generate_factual_qa(self, domain: str, count: int = 100,
                            difficulty: str = "medium") -> list[dict]:
        """
        Generate factual Q&A pairs with verifiable answers.
        Answers are extracted from a trusted knowledge base.
        """
        prompt = f"""
        Generate {count} factual {difficulty}-difficulty questions
        about {domain}. For each question, provide:
        - question: the question text
        - answer: the correct answer (must be verifiable)
        - source: a reference source for the answer
        - distractors: 3 wrong answers for MCQ format
        
        Difficulty guidelines:
        - Easy: basic facts, widely known
        - Medium: detailed facts, requires domain knowledge
        - Hard: obscure facts, requires expert knowledge
        
        Return a JSON array of objects.
        """
        response = self._call_model(prompt)
        try:
            qa_pairs = json.loads(response)
            # Verify answers against knowledge base
            verified = []
            for qa in qa_pairs:
                if self._verify_answer(qa["question"], qa["answer"]):
                    qa["verified"] = True
                    verified.append(qa)
            return verified
        except json.JSONDecodeError:
            return []

    def generate_reasoning(self, count: int = 100,
                           difficulty: str = "medium") -> list[dict]:
        """Generate math/logic reasoning questions."""
        prompt = f"""
        Generate {count} {difficulty} reasoning problems.
        Each must have a unique, deterministic answer.
        Include:
        - problem: the question
        - answer: the final answer (number or short phrase)
        - solution: step-by-step solution
        - difficulty: easy/medium/hard
        
        Return a JSON array.
        """
        response = self._call_model(prompt)
        try:
            return json.loads(response)
        except json.JSONDecodeError:
            return []

    def generate_code(self, count: int = 50,
                      language: str = "python") -> list[dict]:
        """Generate code understanding/generation questions."""
        prompt = f"""
        Generate {count} {language} coding problems.
        Each problem must have:
        - description: problem statement
        - solution: correct code solution
        - test_cases: list of (input, expected_output) pairs
        - difficulty: easy/medium/hard
        
        Ensure all test cases pass with the provided solution.
        Return a JSON array.
        """
        return self._call_model(prompt)

    def _verify_answer(self, question: str, answer: str) -> bool:
        """Verify answer against knowledge base."""
        # In production: query Wikipedia, textbooks, etc.
        return True

    def run_evaluation(self, model_fn, questions: list[dict]
                       ) -> dict:
        """Run a model against the synthetic benchmark."""
        results = []
        correct = 0
        for q in questions:
            model_answer = model_fn(q["question"])
            is_correct = self._check_answer(
                model_answer, q["answer"], q.get("type", "exact")
            )
            results.append({
                "question": q["question"],
                "expected": q["answer"],
                "got": model_answer,
                "correct": is_correct,
                "difficulty": q.get("difficulty", "medium")
            })
            if is_correct:
                correct += 1
        return {
            "total": len(questions),
            "correct": correct,
            "accuracy": correct / max(len(questions), 1),
            "by_difficulty": self._breakdown(results),
            "results": results
        }

    def _check_answer(self, model_answer: str, expected: str,
                      check_type: str) -> bool:
        if check_type == "exact":
            return model_answer.strip().lower() == expected.strip().lower()
        elif check_type == "contains":
            return expected.lower() in model_answer.lower()
        return False

    def _breakdown(self, results: list[dict]) -> dict:
        breakdown = {}
        for r in results:
            diff = r.get("difficulty", "medium")
            if diff not in breakdown:
                breakdown[diff] = {"total": 0, "correct": 0}
            breakdown[diff]["total"] += 1
            if r["correct"]:
                breakdown[diff]["correct"] += 1
        for d in breakdown:
            breakdown[d]["accuracy"] = (
                breakdown[d]["correct"] / max(breakdown[d]["total"], 1)
            )
        return breakdown

    def _call_model(self, prompt: str) -> str:
        return json.dumps([{
            "question": "What is the capital of France?",
            "answer": "Paris",
            "difficulty": "easy"
        }])

sections.title: Avoiding Model Collapse & Distribution Shift
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Model collapse (also called "data contamination" or "the curse of recursion") occurs when a model is trained on data generated by previous versions of itself. The model learns to mimic the distribution of its own outputs, which are already a compressed, lossy representation of the original data. Over successive generations, tail distributions vanish, diversity decreases, and errors compound. The phenomenon is well-documented in generative models (GANs, VAEs) and has been confirmed for LLMs. The Staff insight: model collapse is not a theoretical concern — it's a practical engineering problem whenever you use synthetic data in a training loop. Every synthetic data pipeline must include: (1) fresh real data mixing, (2) diversity monitoring, (3) distributional checks, and (4) data provenance tracking.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Model collapse onset: after 5-10 generations of training purely on synthetic data, perplexity increases by 15-25% (Shumailov et al., 2023). Diversity collapse: unique n-gram count drops 40% after 5 generations. Error amplification: a 1% hallucination rate in the teacher model becomes a 5% hallucination rate in the student after 3 generations of synthetic training. Mixing ratio: 10-30% real data prevents collapse indefinitely. Data provenance: tracking which examples are synthetic vs real, and which generation they belong to, is essential. Remediation: if collapse is detected, roll back to the last mixed-generation checkpoint and re-train with higher real data ratio.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Tag every example with its source: real, synth-v1, synth-v2, etc. 2) Monitor data diversity over time: unique tokens, embedding coverage, n-gram diversity. 3) Track distribution shift between synthetic generations: MMD (Maximum Mean Discrepancy) or KL divergence between synthetic data and held-out real data. 4) Maintain a minimum real-data ratio: 10% for stable domains, 30% for fast-evolving domains. 5) When training or fine-tuning, always include a held-out real validation set for early stopping. 6) If perplexity on real validation data increases, investigate synthetic data quality. 7) For production systems, limit the number of synthetic generation cycles and regenerate from fresh real data periodically.

sections.title: FAANG Q&A — Synthetic Data Generation
sections.description:
search Q1 (Google L6): "Design a synthetic data pipeline for fine-tuning a model for legal document summarisation. You have 200 real lawyer-written summaries." A: Three-stage pipeline. Stage 1 (seed expansion): use GPT-4 to generate 2000 diverse legal document-summary pairs based on the 200 real examples. Control for: document length (1-50 pages), legal domain (contracts, litigation, patents), and summary style (executive, detailed). Stage 2 (quality filtering): train a classifier on the 200 real examples to predict whether a summary is lawyer-quality. Filter synthetic examples that score below 0.8. Stage 3 (validation): have a lawyer review 100 synthetic examples. If agreement rate >90%, proceed to fine-tuning. Mix: 200 real + 2000 synthetic (10% real). Monitor: compare fine-tuned model's summaries against held-out real summaries using ROUGE-L and expert review.

search Q2 (Meta E6): "Your model trained on 50% synthetic data starts showing hallucination regressions. How do you diagnose and fix?" A: Diagnosis: (1) check if the hallucination rate correlates with specific synthetic data batches (data provenance tracking). (2) Compute distribution shift between real and synthetic data (KL divergence, embedding space coverage). (3) Check for model collapse: diversity metrics on synthetic data generations. Fix: (1) increase real data ratio from 50% to 80%. (2) Filter synthetic data more aggressively using a factuality classifier. (3) Re-generate synthetic data with a better teacher model (GPT-4 -> human expert). (4) Add a factuality verification step to the generation pipeline (extract claims, verify against knowledge base). (5) Re-train with early stopping on a real validation set.

search Q3 (Anthropic L5): "How do you generate synthetic data for a task that requires expertise beyond the teacher model's capabilities?" A: Weak-to-strong generation. Use the teacher model to generate candidate examples, then use a combination of: (1) expert-in-the-loop: have domain experts review and correct synthetic examples, creating a small set of high-quality examples that the student model learns from. (2) Verifier-guided generation: for tasks with verifiable outputs (code, math), use an automated verifier to filter correct examples — the verifier doesn't need to solve the task, just check correctness. (3) Scarce-data bootstrapping: generate synthetic examples for the subtasks the teacher CAN do, then use a human to generate only the hard cases. (4) Ensemble generation: combine outputs from multiple weaker models to approximate expert quality. At Anthropic, we use Constitutional AI: the model generates, critiques, and revises its own outputs, bootstrapping quality beyond its initial capability.

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) (Rafailov et al., 2023) — Training alignment without RL, works well with synthetic preference data.
  • Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback (Bai et al., 2022) — Anthropic's method for generating synthetic preference pairs via self-critique.
  • The Curse of Recursion: Training on Generated Data Makes Models Forget (Shumailov et al., 2023) — Foundational paper on model collapse.
  • Synthetic Data in AI: Challenges, Methods, and Future Directions (Joshi et al., 2024) — Comprehensive survey of synthetic data techniques.
  • Hugging Face Alignment Handbook — Practical recipes for RLHF/DPO alignment with synthetic data.
  • Self-Play Fine-Tuning (SPIN) (Chen et al., 2024) — Using the model's own generations to improve alignment.

details:
Synthetic Data Decision Flow: Start with use case definition (alignment, evaluation, augmentation). Design generation prompts with diverse seeds and controlled difficulty. Generate with a strong teacher model (GPT-4o, Claude 3.5). Filter for quality (judge model, verifier, diversity metrics). Mix with real data (10-30% real). Monitor for collapse (diversity trends, distribution shift). Validate with held-out real test set. Iterate: improve generation pipeline based on validation results. At FAANG scale, synthetic data is not a shortcut — it's a sophisticated engineering discipline with its own quality metrics, pipeline architecture, and failure modes.

Related: Advanced Agent Architectures | LLM Security & Guardrails | LLM Observability
